rationale: The cited paper is used in the citing paper's theory development, not just as a background list item. In the theoretical section, the authors explicitly rely on Barasch and Berger (2014) to support the claim that broadcasting contexts increase impression-management motives in sharing. That logic is then used to motivate predictions about how different overlap-based audience structures (especially common followers vs common mutual followers) affect rebroadcasting. So the citation plays a meaningful conceptual framing role even without direct method reuse.
relation_description: The citing paper uses Barasch and Berger's broadcasting-versus-narrowcasting insight as a conceptual basis for its mechanism about audience-related sharing motives. It builds its network-overlap hypotheses on that audience/impression-management logic in a directed social-media setting.
